figures.caption: Table 2: In-distribution early-stopping performance on the 5K test set (Qwen2.5-32B, ϵ=0.05). TTT-Probe (no-QK) improves savings by 24.9% relative over the static baseline.
Table 9: Step-level vs. token-level savings (δ=0.1, supervised). The two metrics are highly consistent across models.
Table 3: OOD generalization at δ=0.1. The TTT-Probe achieves 2.6–2.7× the baseline savings on MATH-500 under supervised labels.
Figure 1: Framework of Online Reasoning Calibration (ORCA).
Figure 3: Actual error rate vs. target risk δ (supervised, Qwen2.5-32B). All methods track the diagonal, confirming valid risk control. Points below the diagonal satisfy the LTT guarantee.

Table 5: Core mechanism ablation (supervised, δ=0.1). Standard supervised training on the same architecture underperforms the static baseline (no-QK) or only matches it (QK). TTT meta-learning with online updates is necessary for substantial improvement. Rows marked with ∗ use random initialization.

Figure 5: Probe score trajectories for a test problem (Qwen2.5-32B, δ=0.1). The green line marks the first correct step. The static probe (top) never crosses its threshold and saves 0%. The TTT no-QK probe (bottom) crosses the threshold at step 22 and saves 41%.

Figure 2: Compute savings vs. risk tolerance δ for supervised (left) and consistent (right) labels (Qwen2.5-32B). TTT no-QK consistently outperforms the baseline across all risk levels, with the largest gap at low δ.
